title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
description: Find median of two sorted arrays in logarithmic time

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize merged=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, 1<=2, so append 1 to merged: merged=[1], i=1. Step 3: i=1 within nums1 length 2, j=0 within nums2 length 1. Compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[0]=2, 3>2, so append 2 to merged: merged=[1,2], j=1. Step 4: j=1 equals nums2 length 1, so exit while loop. Step 5: Append remaining nums1[i:]=[3] to merged: merged=[1,2,3]. Step 6: n=3, odd, so median = merged[3//2]=merged[1]=2.0.

Step 1: Initialize merged=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=3, 1<=3, so append 1 to merged: merged=[1], i=1. Step 3: i=1 within nums1 length 2, j=0 within nums2 length 2. Compare nums1[1]=2 and nums2[0]=3, 2<=3, so append 2 to merged: merged=[1,2], i=2. Step 4: i=2 equals nums1 length 2, so exit while loop. Step 5: Append remaining nums2[j:]=[3,4] to merged: merged=[1,2,3,4]. Step 6: n=4, even, so median = (merged[4//2-1]+merged[4//2])/2.0 = (merged[1]+merged[2])/2.0 = (2+3)/2.0 = 2.5.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Merge O(m+n)
approaches.summary: Merge the two sorted arrays into one sorted array, then find the median by accessing the middle element(s). This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Median of Two Sorted Arrays via Brute Force Merge
ladders.desc: Complete solution: merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array, then find the median.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the problem by first merging the two sorted input arrays into a single sorted array, then computing the median from this merged array. The intuition is straightforward: since both arrays are already sorted, we can efficiently combine them using a two-pointer merge technique similar to the merge step in merge sort. Once merged, finding the median is trivial: if the total number of elements is odd, the median is the middle element; if even, it's the average of the two middle elements. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty array 'merged' and two pointers i=0 for nums1 and j=0 for nums2. 2) While both pointers are within bounds, compare nums1[i] and nums2[j], append the smaller to merged, and increment that pointer. 3) After one array is exhausted, append all remaining elements from the other array to merged. 4) Let n = len(merged). If n is odd, return merged[n//2]; if even, return (merged[n//2-1] + merged[n//2])/2.0. Time complexity is O(m+n) where m and n are lengths of the input arrays, as we traverse each element once. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the merged array. This feeds directly into the problem by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty arrays (should return median of the non-empty array), incorrect median calculation for even lengths (must average two middle elements), and off-by-one errors in indices. The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def findMedianSortedArrays(nums1, nums2): merged = []; i=j=0; while i<len(nums1) and j<len(nums2): if nums1[i]<=nums2[j]: merged.append(nums1[i]); i+=1 else: merged.append(nums2[j]); j+=1; merged.extend(nums1[i:]); merged.extend(nums2[j:]); n=len(merged); if n%2==1: return merged[n//2]; else: return (merged[n//2-1]+merged[n//2])/2.0.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize merged=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2, 1<=2, so append 1 to merged: merged=[1], i=1. Step 3: i=1 within nums1 length 2, j=0 within nums2 length 1. Compare nums1[1]=3 and nums2[0]=2, 3>2, so append 2 to merged: merged=[1,2], j=1. Step 4: j=1 equals nums2 length 1, so exit while loop. Step 5: Append remaining nums1[i:]=[3] to merged: merged=[1,2,3]. Step 6: n=3, odd, so median = merged[3//2]=merged[1]=2.0.

Step 1: Initialize merged=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=3, 1<=3, so append 1 to merged: merged=[1], i=1. Step 3: i=1 within nums1 length 2, j=0 within nums2 length 2. Compare nums1[1]=2 and nums2[0]=3, 2<=3, so append 2 to merged: merged=[1,2], i=2. Step 4: i=2 equals nums1 length 2, so exit while loop. Step 5: Append remaining nums2[j:]=[3,4] to merged: merged=[1,2,3,4]. Step 6: n=4, even, so median = (merged[4//2-1]+merged[4//2])/2.0 = (merged[1]+merged[2])/2.0 = (2+3)/2.0 = 2.5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array using two-pointer technique.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach: it efficiently combines two sorted arrays into a single sorted array. The intuition leverages the fact that both arrays are sorted, so we can compare the smallest remaining elements from each array and always pick the smaller one, ensuring the merged array stays sorted. This is similar to the merge step in merge sort. Step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list 'result' and two pointers i=0 for array A and j=0 for array B. 2) While i < len(A) and j < len(B): compare A[i] and B[j]. If A[i] <= B[j], append A[i] to result and increment i; otherwise, append B[j] to result and increment j. 3) After the loop, one array may have remaining elements. Append all remaining elements from A[i:] and B[j:] to result (only one will have elements). 4) Return result. Time complexity is O(m+n) as each element is processed once. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the result array. This feeds into L0 by providing the merged array needed for median calculation. Common mistakes include: not handling empty arrays correctly (the algorithm works as pointers won't enter the loop), using inefficient methods like sorting after concatenation (O((m+n)log(m+n)) time), and off-by-one errors in pointer increments. Real code pattern: def mergeSortedArrays(A, B): result=[]; i=j=0; while i<len(A) and j<len(B): if A[i]<=B[j]: result.append(A[i]); i+=1 else: result.append(B[j]); j+=1; result.extend(A[i:]); result.extend(B[j:]); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: result=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: i=0<3, j=0<3. Compare A[0]=1 and B[0]=2, 1<=2, append 1: result=[1], i=1. Step 3: i=1<3, j=0<3. Compare A[1]=3 and B[0]=2, 3>2, append 2: result=[1,2], j=1. Step 4: i=1<3, j=1<3. Compare A[1]=3 and B[1]=4, 3<=4, append 3: result=[1,2,3], i=2. Step 5: i=2<3, j=1<3. Compare A[2]=5 and B[1]=4, 5>4, append 4: result=[1,2,3,4], j=2. Step 6: i=2<3, j=2<3. Compare A[2]=5 and B[2]=6, 5<=6, append 5: result=[1,2,3,4,5], i=3. Step 7: i=3 equals len(A), exit while loop. Step 8: Append remaining B[j:]=[6] to result: result=[1,2,3,4,5,6].

Step 1: result=[], i=0, j=0. Step 2: i=0<2, j=0<3. Compare A[0]=10 and B[0]=15, 10<=15, append 10: result=[10], i=1. Step 3: i=1<2, j=0<3. Compare A[1]=20 and B[0]=15, 20>15, append 15: result=[10,15], j=1. Step 4: i=1<2, j=1<3. Compare A[1]=20 and B[1]=25, 20<=25, append 20: result=[10,15,20], i=2. Step 5: i=2 equals len(A), exit while loop. Step 6: Append remaining B[j:]=[25,30] to result: result=[10,15,20,25,30].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Pointer Comparison in Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Use two pointers to traverse two sorted arrays simultaneously, comparing elements to maintain order.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using two indices (pointers) to iterate through two sorted arrays, comparing elements at these pointers to decide which one to process next. The intuition is that since both arrays are sorted, the smallest unprocessed element overall must be at one of the two pointer positions. By always picking the smaller of the two, we can build a merged sorted list efficiently. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize pointer i for array A and pointer j for array B, both at 0. 2) In a loop, while both pointers are within bounds: compare A[i] and B[j]. If A[i] <= B[j], process A[i] (e.g., append to a result list) and increment i; otherwise, process B[j] and increment j. 3) The loop ends when one pointer goes out of bounds, meaning all elements from that array have been processed. This technique ensures linear time traversal. Time complexity is O(m+n) in the worst case, as each pointer moves at most m or n times. Space complexity is O(1) extra space if not counting output storage. This feeds into L1 by providing the mechanism to merge arrays without extra sorting. Common mistakes include: using while i < len(A) or j < len(B) instead of 'and' (which would skip elements), not incrementing pointers correctly, and comparing elements without handling equal values properly (using <= ensures stability). Real code pattern: i=j=0; while i<len(A) and j<len(B): if A[i]<=B[j]: process A[i]; i+=1 else: process B[j]; j+=1.

examples.input: A = [1,4,7], B = [2,5,8], process = append to result
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0, result=[]. Step 2: i=0<3, j=0<3. Compare A[0]=1 and B[0]=2, 1<=2, append 1: result=[1], i=1. Step 3: i=1<3, j=0<3. Compare A[1]=4 and B[0]=2, 4>2, append 2: result=[1,2], j=1. Step 4: i=1<3, j=1<3. Compare A[1]=4 and B[1]=5, 4<=5, append 4: result=[1,2,4], i=2. Step 5: i=2<3, j=1<3. Compare A[2]=7 and B[1]=5, 7>5, append 5: result=[1,2,4,5], j=2. Step 6: i=2<3, j=2<3. Compare A[2]=7 and B[2]=8, 7<=8, append 7: result=[1,2,4,5,7], i=3. Step 7: i=3 equals len(A), exit loop. Result after loop is [1,2,4,5,7], but trace shows up to step 5 for demonstration: result=[1,2,4,5], i=2, j=2.

examples.input: A = [3,6], B = [1,4,5], process = append to result
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0, result=[]. Step 2: i=0<2, j=0<3. Compare A[0]=3 and B[0]=1, 3>1, append 1: result=[1], j=1. Step 3: i=0<2, j=1<3. Compare A[0]=3 and B[1]=4, 3<=4, append 3: result=[1,3], i=1. Step 4: i=1<2, j=1<3. Compare A[1]=6 and B[1]=4, 6>4, append 4: result=[1,3,4], j=2. Step 5: i=1<2, j=2<3. Compare A[1]=6 and B[2]=5, 6>5, append 5: result=[1,3,4,5], j=3. Step 6: j=3 equals len(B), exit loop. Result after loop is [1,3,4,5], but trace shows up to step 4 for demonstration: result=[1,3,4], i=1, j=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Arrays with Pointers
ladders.desc: Use index pointers to traverse arrays sequentially and access elements.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using integer variables as pointers (indices) to access elements in arrays sequentially. The intuition is that arrays are indexed starting from 0, and by incrementing a pointer, we can move through the array one element at a time. This is fundamental for processing arrays in loops. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a pointer (e.g., i) to 0 to start at the first element. 2) Use a loop (e.g., while or for) to continue as long as i is less than the length of the array. 3) Inside the loop, access the element at array[i], perform some operation, then increment i by 1 to move to the next element. 4) After the loop, i will equal the array length, indicating all elements have been processed. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing an array of length n. Space complexity is O(1) for the pointer variable. This feeds into L2 by providing the basic mechanism to move through two arrays simultaneously with separate pointers. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (e.g., using i <= len(array) which causes index out of bounds), forgetting to increment the pointer (leading to infinite loops), and using pointers beyond array bounds without checks. Real code pattern: i=0; while i<len(arr): access arr[i]; i+=1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0. Step 2: Check i<3? Yes, print arr[0]=10, increment i to 1. Step 3: Check i<3? Yes, print arr[1]=20, increment i to 2. Step 4: Check i<3? Yes, print arr[2]=30, increment i to 3. Step 5: Check i<3? No, exit loop. Printed: 10,20,30.

Step 1: Initialize i=0, sum=0. Step 2: Check i<4? Yes, sum+=arr[0]=5, sum=5, increment i to 1. Step 3: Check i<4? Yes, sum+=arr[1]=15, sum=20, increment i to 2. Step 4: Check i<4? Yes, sum+=arr[2]=25, sum=45, increment i to 3. Step 5: Check i<4? Yes, sum+=arr[3]=35, sum=80, increment i to 4. Step 6: Check i<4? No, exit loop. Sum=80.

testCases.input: arr = [9,1,5], operation = sort manually (not needed for trace)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve an element from an array using its position (index).
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing a specific element in an array by its index. In programming, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The intuition is that an array is a contiguous block of memory, and the index acts as an offset from the start to quickly locate any element. To access an element: use the syntax array[index], where index is an integer between 0 and len(array)-1 inclusive. If the index is out of this range, it results in an error (e.g., IndexError in Python). This operation is constant time, O(1), as it directly computes the memory location. Space complexity is O(1) as no extra space is needed. This feeds into L3 by enabling the retrieval of elements during iteration. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing (e.g., array[1] for the first element), accessing with negative indices without understanding (in some languages, -1 refers to the last element), and not checking bounds before access. Real code pattern: element = arr[i] where i is a valid index.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array arr has elements: index 0:10, index 1:20, index 2:30, index 3:40. Step 2: Access arr[2]. Since index 2 corresponds to the third element, the value is 30.
Step 1: Array arr has elements: index 0:5, index 1:15, index 2:25. Step 2: Access arr[0]. Since index 0 corresponds to the first element, the value is 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Array?
ladders.desc: An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by index.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations. The intuition is that it's like a row of boxes, each holding a value, with positions numbered starting from 0. This allows efficient access to any element by its index, as the memory address can be computed directly from the base address and index offset. Key properties: 1) Elements are stored sequentially. 2) Each element has a unique index (0-based). 3) The size is fixed upon creation (in many languages). 4) Access time is O(1). Arrays are used to store lists of items, such as numbers or strings, and are building blocks for more complex algorithms. This feeds into L4 by providing the structure on which indexing operates. Common misconceptions include: confusing arrays with lists (lists may be dynamic), thinking indices start at 1, and not understanding memory contiguity. Real code pattern: In Python, a list is used as an array-like structure: arr = [1,2,3] creates an array with elements 1,2,3 at indices 0,1,2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Allocate memory for 3 elements. Step 2: Store 5 at position 0 (first box). Step 3: Store 10 at position 1 (second box). Step 4: Store 15 at position 2 (third box). Resulting array: values [5,10,15] with indices mapping 0->5, 1->10, 2->15.
Step 1: Visualize array as boxes: index 0:2, index 1:4, index 2:6, index 3:8. Step 2: Index 2 refers to the third box (since counting starts at 0). Step 3: The value in the third box is 6.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Binary Search Partition O(log(min(m,n)))
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the smaller array to partition both arrays such that elements on the left are less than or equal to those on the right, then compute the median from the partition boundaries. This achieves logarithmic time without merging.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Median of Two Sorted Arrays via Binary Search Partition
ladders.desc: Complete logarithmic-time solution using binary search on the smaller array to partition both arrays and compute median.
ladders.explanation: This solution finds the median of two sorted arrays without merging them, achieving O(log(min(m,n))) time complexity. The core insight is that we can partition both arrays such that all elements on the left side of the partition are less than or equal to all elements on the right side. We perform binary search on the smaller array to find this optimal partition point. For arrays nums1 (size m) and nums2 (size n), we ensure m ≤ n. We search for partition index i in nums1 (0 to m) and calculate j = (m+n+1)//2 - i in nums2. The partition divides both arrays: left side contains nums1[0:i] and nums2[0:j], right side contains nums1[i:m] and nums2[j:n]. We need to satisfy: maxLeft1 ≤ minRight2 AND maxLeft2 ≤ minRight1. If not, we adjust i using binary search. Once found, median calculation depends on total length parity: if odd, median = max(maxLeft1, maxLeft2); if even, median = (max(maxLeft1, maxLeft2) + min(minRight1, minRight2))/2. Time: O(log(min(m,n))) from binary search on smaller array. Space: O(1) using only pointers. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into complete solution. Common mistakes: Not handling edge cases (empty arrays, single elements), incorrect partition index calculation, off-by-one errors in median calculation. Real code pattern: def findMedianSortedArrays(nums1, nums2): if len(nums1) > len(nums2): nums1, nums2 = nums2, nums1; m, n = len(nums1), len(nums2); low, high = 0, m; while low <= high: i = (low+high)//2; j = (m+n+1)//2 - i; maxLeft1 = float('-inf') if i==0 else nums1[i-1]; minRight1 = float('inf') if i==m else nums1[i]; maxLeft2 = float('-inf') if j==0 else nums2[j-1]; minRight2 = float('inf') if j==n else nums2[j]; if maxLeft1 <= minRight2 and maxLeft2 <= minRight1: if (m+n)%2==1: return max(maxLeft1, maxLeft2); else: return (max(maxLeft1, maxLeft2)+min(minRight1, minRight2))/2; elif maxLeft1 > minRight2: high = i-1; else: low = i+1; return 0.0

examples.trace: Step 1: Ensure nums1 is smaller: nums1=[1,3] (m=2), nums2=[2] (n=1). Step 2: Initialize low=0, high=2. Step 3: Binary search iteration 1: i=(0+2)//2=1, j=(2+1+1)//2-1=1 (since (m+n+1)//2=(3)//2=1). Partition: nums1 left=[1], right=[3]; nums2 left=[2], right=[]. Values: maxLeft1=nums1[0]=1, minRight1=nums1[1]=3, maxLeft2=nums2[0]=2, minRight2=inf. Condition check: maxLeft1(1)≤minRight2(inf) ✓, maxLeft2(2)≤minRight1(3) ✓. Condition satisfied. Step 4: Total length m+n=3 (odd), median=max(maxLeft1,maxLeft2)=max(1,2)=2.0.

Step 1: Ensure nums1 is smaller: nums1=[1,2] (m=2), nums2=[3,4] (n=2). Step 2: Initialize low=0, high=2. Step 3: Binary search iteration 1: i=(0+2)//2=1, j=(2+2+1)//2-1=2 (since (m+n+1)//2=(5)//2=2). Partition: nums1 left=[1], right=[2]; nums2 left=[3,4], right=[]. Values: maxLeft1=1, minRight1=2, maxLeft2=4, minRight2=inf. Condition: maxLeft1(1)≤minRight2(inf) ✓, maxLeft2(4)≤minRight1(2) ✗ (4>2). Since maxLeft2>minRight1, need more elements on left side of nums2, so increase i: low=i+1=2. Step 4: Iteration 2: i=(2+2)//2=2, j=2-2=0. Partition: nums1 left=[1,2], right=[]; nums2 left=[], right=[3,4]. Values: maxLeft1=2, minRight1=inf, maxLeft2=-inf, minRight2=3. Condition: maxLeft1(2)≤minRight2(3) ✓, maxLeft2(-inf)≤minRight1(inf) ✓. Condition satisfied. Step 5: Total length m+n=4 (even), median=(max(maxLeft1,maxLeft2)+min(minRight1,minRight2))/2=(max(2,-inf)+min(inf,3))/2=(2+3)/2=2.5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search for Optimal Partition Index
ladders.desc: Find partition index i in smaller array such that left partition elements ≤ right partition elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the binary search to find the correct partition index i in the smaller array (nums1). Given arrays nums1 (size m) and nums2 (size n) with m ≤ n, we search i in range [0, m]. For each candidate i, we compute j = (m+n+1)//2 - i to partition nums2. This ensures total left partition size = (m+n+1)//2 (handling odd/even totals). We check if max(nums1[i-1], nums2[j-1]) ≤ min(nums1[i], nums2[j]) (with boundary checks). If maxLeft1 > minRight2, i is too large → move left (high=i-1). If maxLeft2 > minRight1, i is too small → move right (low=i+1). When condition satisfied, i is optimal. Time: O(log m) from binary search on nums1. Space: O(1). Connection: This finds the partition needed for median calculation in L0. Common mistakes: Incorrect j calculation, not handling array boundaries properly, infinite loops in binary search. Real code pattern: def findPartitionIndex(nums1, nums2): m, n = len(nums1), len(nums2); low, high = 0, m; while low <= high: i = (low+high)//2; j = (m+n+1)//2 - i; maxLeft1 = float('-inf') if i==0 else nums1[i-1]; minRight1 = float('inf') if i==m else nums1[i]; maxLeft2 = float('-inf') if j==0 else nums2[j-1]; minRight2 = float('inf') if j==n else nums2[j]; if maxLeft1 <= minRight2 and maxLeft2 <= minRight1: return i, j; elif maxLeft1 > minRight2: high = i-1; else: low = i+1; return -1, -1

examples.trace: Step 1: m=2, n=1, low=0, high=2. Step 2: Iteration 1: i=(0+2)//2=1, j=(2+1+1)//2-1=1. Compute: maxLeft1=nums1[0]=1, minRight1=nums1[1]=3, maxLeft2=nums2[0]=2, minRight2=inf. Condition: 1≤inf ✓, 2≤3 ✓ → satisfied. Return i=1, j=1.

Step 1: m=2, n=2, low=0, high=2. Step 2: Iteration 1: i=1, j=(2+2+1)//2-1=2. Compute: maxLeft1=1, minRight1=2, maxLeft2=nums2[1]=4, minRight2=inf. Condition: 1≤inf ✓, 4≤2 ✗ → maxLeft2>minRight1, so low=i+1=2. Step 3: Iteration 2: i=(2+2)//2=2, j=2-2=0. Compute: maxLeft1=nums1[1]=2, minRight1=inf, maxLeft2=-inf, minRight2=nums2[0]=3. Condition: 2≤3 ✓, -inf≤inf ✓ → satisfied. Return i=2, j=0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Partition Condition for Median Finding
ladders.desc: Understand the condition that ensures a partition gives correct median: left partition elements ≤ right partition elements.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that for a sorted combined array, the median depends on the middle elements. If we partition both arrays at indices i and j such that all elements on the left are ≤ all elements on the right, then the boundary elements (max of left, min of right) are candidates for median. Specifically, for arrays A and B partitioned at i and j, we need: A[i-1] ≤ B[j] AND B[j-1] ≤ A[i]. This ensures the left partition contains the first half of combined sorted elements. Why? In a sorted combined array, elements are interleaved from A and B. This condition guarantees no element from left partition is greater than any element from right partition, meaning the partition correctly splits the combined array. For median calculation: if total length odd, median = max(A[i-1], B[j-1]); if even, median = (max(A[i-1], B[j-1]) + min(A[i], B[j]))/2. Time: O(1) to check condition. Space: O(1). Connection: This condition is checked in L1's binary search. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle empty partitions (use -inf/inf), misunderstanding the inequality direction. Real code pattern: def isPartitionValid(A, i, B, j): maxLeftA = -inf if i==0 else A[i-1]; minRightA = inf if i==len(A) else A[i]; maxLeftB = -inf if j==0 else B[j-1]; minRightB = inf if j==len(B) else B[j]; return maxLeftA <= minRightB and maxLeftB <= minRightA

examples.input: A=[1,3], i=1, B=[2], j=1, check condition
examples.trace: Step 1: Compute values: maxLeftA=A[0]=1, minRightA=A[1]=3, maxLeftB=B[0]=2, minRightB=inf. Step 2: Check: 1 ≤ inf ✓, 2 ≤ 3 ✓. Condition satisfied → True.

examples.input: A=[1,2], i=1, B=[3,4], j=2, check condition
examples.trace: Step 1: Compute: maxLeftA=1, minRightA=2, maxLeftB=B[1]=4, minRightB=inf. Step 2: Check: 1 ≤ inf ✓, 4 ≤ 2 ✗. Condition failed → False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Partition Index j from i
ladders.desc: Calculate j in larger array given i in smaller array to maintain equal left/right partition sizes.
ladders.explanation: Given partition index i in the smaller array (size m), we compute j in the larger array (size n) as j = (m+n+1)//2 - i. This ensures the total number of elements in the left partition (i + j) equals (m+n+1)//2. The +1 handles both odd and even total lengths: for odd total length, left partition has one more element; for even, both partitions have equal elements. Why? In a sorted combined array of length L, median depends on elements at positions around L//2. By making left partition size = (L+1)//2, we guarantee max of left partition is the median for odd L, or one of two medians for even L. This formula works because i ranges from 0 to m, and j will be in range 0 to n when i is optimal. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: This j is used in L2's partition condition. Common mistakes: Forgetting integer division, using wrong formula (e.g., (m+n)//2 - i), not ensuring j is non-negative. Real code pattern: def computeJ(m, n, i): return (m + n + 1) // 2 - i

examples.trace: Step 1: Total length L=m+n=3. Step 2: Left partition target size = (L+1)//2 = (3+1)//2 = 4//2 = 2. Step 3: j = target - i = 2 - 1 = 1. Step 4: Verify: i+j=1+1=2 equals target.
Step 1: L=4. Step 2: Target = (4+1)//2 = 5//2 = 2. Step 3: j = 2 - 2 = 0. Step 4: Verify: i+j=2+0=2 equals target.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Elements Around Partition with Boundary Checks
ladders.desc: Safely get max of left partition and min of right partition for an array given partition index.
ladders.explanation: For an array A and partition index i (where i indicates split between A[0:i] left and A[i:] right), we need to access A[i-1] (max of left) and A[i] (min of right). However, i can be 0 (left empty) or len(A) (right empty), causing index errors. We handle this by using sentinel values: if i==0, maxLeft = -infinity; if i==len(A), minRight = +infinity. This ensures comparisons work correctly in partition condition. Why? In binary search, we consider all possible i from 0 to m. When i=0, left partition of A is empty, so no element from A contributes to max left—treat as -inf. When i=m, right partition is empty, so no element from A contributes to min right—treat as +inf. This allows condition checks without errors. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: This operation is used in L3 to get values for condition check. Common mistakes: Not handling boundaries, using wrong sentinel values, off-by-one in indices. Real code pattern: def getPartitionValues(A, i): maxLeft = float('-inf') if i==0 else A[i-1]; minRight = float('inf') if i==len(A) else A[i]; return maxLeft, minRight

examples.trace: Step 1: i=1, len(A)=2. Step 2: Since i≠0, maxLeft=A[i-1]=A[0]=1. Step 3: Since i≠len(A), minRight=A[i]=A[1]=3.
Step 1: i=2, len(A)=2. Step 2: i≠0, maxLeft=A[1]=2. Step 3: i==len(A), so minRight=inf.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Median of a Single Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Find median of one sorted array, understanding odd/even length cases.
ladders.explanation: The median is the middle value in a sorted list. For an array A of length L: if L is odd, median = A[L//2] (middle element); if L is even, median = (A[L//2 - 1] + A[L//2]) / 2 (average of two middle elements). Indexing starts at 0. Why? In a sorted array, elements are in ascending order. The median splits the data into two halves: for odd L, there's one exact middle; for even L, two middle elements represent center. This is foundational because for two arrays, we aim to find similar middle elements in combined sorted order. Time: O(1) to compute. Space: O(1). Connection: This basic median calculation is extended in L4 to handle two arrays via partition. Common mistakes: Using wrong indices (off-by-one), forgetting to handle even case, not using float division for even case. Real code pattern: def medianSingleArray(A): L = len(A); if L%2==1: return A[L//2]; else: return (A[L//2 - 1] + A[L//2]) / 2

examples.trace: Step 1: L=3 (odd). Step 2: Median index = L//2 = 3//2 = 1. Step 3: A[1]=2.0.
Step 1: L=4 (even). Step 2: Middle indices: L//2 - 1 = 1, L//2 = 2. Step 3: A[1]=2, A[2]=3. Step 4: Median = (2+3)/2 = 2.5.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Two Pointer O(m+n) with O(1) Space
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to traverse both arrays simultaneously to find the median without storing a merged array. This reduces space usage compared to brute force but retains linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Two Pointer Median Finder
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find median of two sorted arrays using two pointers without merging arrays.
ladders.explanation: This solution finds the median of two sorted arrays in O(m+n) time with O(1) space by using two pointers to traverse both arrays simultaneously. The intuition is that we don't need to physically merge the arrays—we can simulate the merging process by tracking which elements would appear in the merged order. We maintain two pointers (i for nums1, j for nums2) and a counter to track how many elements we've processed. We advance the pointer pointing to the smaller element, effectively simulating the sorted merge. For odd total length, we need the middle element; for even, we need the average of two middle elements. We track previous and current elements to handle even-length cases. The algorithm: 1) Calculate total length and median positions. 2) Initialize pointers and counters. 3) While counter hasn't reached median position: compare nums1[i] and nums2[j], advance pointer with smaller element, store previous element. 4) Handle odd/even cases. Time: O(m+n) as we traverse at most m+n elements. Space: O(1) using only pointers and counters. This feeds into the full problem by implementing the complete two-pointer approach. Common mistakes: forgetting to handle empty arrays, off-by-one errors in median position calculation, not tracking previous element for even case. Real code pattern: while counter < median_pos: if i < m and (j >= n or nums1[i] < nums2[j]): current = nums1[i]; i += 1 else: current = nums2[j]; j += 1; if counter == median_pos-1: prev = current; counter += 1; return median based on total length parity.

examples.trace: Total length = 3, median position = 2 (1-indexed). i=0, j=0, counter=0. nums1[0]=1 < nums2[0]=2 → current=1, i=1, counter=1. nums1[1]=3 > nums2[0]=2 → current=2, j=1, counter=2 (reached median position). prev=1. Total odd → median = current = 2.0.

Total length = 4, median positions = 2,3 (1-indexed). i=0, j=0, counter=0. nums1[0]=1 < nums2[0]=3 → current=1, i=1, counter=1. nums1[1]=2 < nums2[0]=3 → current=2, i=2, counter=2 (first median position). prev=1. Continue: nums2[0]=3 < nums1 out → current=3, j=1, counter=3 (second median position). Total even → median = (prev + current)/2 = (2 + 3)/2 = 2.5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two Pointer Merge Simulation
ladders.desc: Simulate merging two sorted arrays using pointers to find k-th smallest element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine finds the k-th smallest element (1-indexed) in two sorted arrays using two pointers without merging. The intuition: since arrays are sorted, we can traverse them in order by always picking the smaller current element. We maintain pointers i and j, and advance the one with smaller value, counting how many elements we've processed. When counter reaches k, we've found the k-th smallest. Algorithm: 1) Initialize i=0, j=0, counter=0. 2) While counter < k: if i < len(nums1) and (j >= len(nums2) or nums1[i] < nums2[j]): current = nums1[i]; i += 1 else: current = nums2[j]; j += 1; counter += 1. 3) Return current. Time: O(k) ≤ O(m+n). Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the core mechanism to find median positions. Common mistakes: not handling array bounds correctly, off-by-one in counter. Real code pattern: while counter < k: if i < m and (j >= n or nums1[i] < nums2[j]): result = nums1[i]; i += 1 else: result = nums2[j]; j += 1; counter += 1; return result.

examples.trace: i=0, j=0, counter=0. nums1[0]=1 < nums2[0]=2 → current=1, i=1, counter=1. nums2[0]=2 < nums1[1]=3 → current=2, j=1, counter=2. nums1[1]=3 < nums2[1]=4 → current=3, i=2, counter=3 (reached k). Return 3.

i=0, j=0, counter=0. nums2[0]=5 < nums1[0]=10 → current=5, j=1, counter=1. nums2[1]=15 > nums1[0]=10 → current=10, i=1, counter=2. nums1[1]=20 > nums2[1]=15 → current=15, j=2, counter=3. nums1 out, nums2 out → but i=1 still valid: current=20, i=2, counter=4. Return 20.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pointer Advancement Strategy
ladders.desc: Algorithm to decide which pointer to advance when traversing two sorted arrays.
ladders.explanation: The core logic determines which array's pointer to advance when simulating merge. Intuition: since both arrays are sorted, the next smallest element overall must be the smaller of the two current elements (nums1[i] and nums2[j]), or from one array if the other is exhausted. We compare nums1[i] and nums2[j]: if nums1[i] < nums2[j], advance i; else advance j. If one array is exhausted, advance the other. This ensures we process elements in sorted order. Algorithm: if i < len(nums1) and (j >= len(nums2) or nums1[i] < nums2[j]): process nums1[i]; i += 1 else: process nums2[j]; j += 1. Time: O(1) per comparison. Space: O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the decision mechanism for merge simulation. Common mistakes: not checking array bounds first, incorrect comparison order leading to index errors. Real code pattern: if i < m and (j >= n or nums1[i] < nums2[j]): val = nums1[i]; i += 1 else: val = nums2[j]; j += 1.

examples.trace: i=0 (< len(nums1)=2), j=0 (< len(nums2)=2). Compare nums1[0]=1 and nums2[0]=2. Since 1 < 2, condition nums1[i] < nums2[j] is true. So advance i to 1.
i=0 (< len(nums1)=1), j=1 (< len(nums2)=2). Compare nums1[0]=5 and nums2[1]=3. Since 5 > 3, condition nums1[i] < nums2[j] is false. So advance j to 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Median Position Calculation
ladders.desc: Calculate which positions in merged array correspond to median based on total length.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the positions (1-indexed) we need to find for the median. Intuition: for an array of length n, if n is odd, median is element at position (n+1)/2; if n is even, median is average of elements at positions n/2 and n/2+1. For two arrays with lengths m and n, total length = m+n. We need to find these positions in the virtual merged array. Algorithm: total = m+n; if total % 2 == 1: median_pos = (total+1)//2 (single position); else: median_pos1 = total//2, median_pos2 = total//2 + 1 (two positions). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This feeds into L2 by telling the pointer advancement how many steps to take. Common mistakes: using 0-indexing incorrectly, off-by-one in even case. Real code pattern: total = m + n; if total % 2: pos = total // 2 + 1; else: pos1 = total // 2; pos2 = total // 2 + 1.

examples.trace: Total length = 2+1 = 3. 3 is odd. Median position = (3+1)/2 = 2 (1-indexed).
Total length = 2+2 = 4. 4 is even. Median positions = total/2 = 2 and total/2+1 = 3 (1-indexed).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Select Smaller Element
ladders.desc: Compare two numbers and select the smaller one, handling cases where one might not exist.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation compares two elements from potentially different arrays and selects the smaller, with special handling if one array is exhausted. Intuition: when traversing two arrays, we need to pick the next smallest element. If both arrays have elements, compare them directly. If one array is exhausted, we must pick from the other. Algorithm: if a is available and (b is not available or a < b): select a; else: select b. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the fundamental comparison for pointer advancement. Common mistakes: not checking availability before comparison, incorrect operator precedence. Real code pattern: if i < len(arr1) and (j >= len(arr2) or arr1[i] < arr2[j]): select arr1[i] else: select arr2[j].

examples.trace: Both available. Compare 5 and 3. Since 5 < 3 is false, condition a < b is false. So select b=3.
a available, b not available. Condition 'b is not available' is true, so overall condition is true. Select a=2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Array Pointers and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic concept of using indices to access array elements and traverse arrays.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers how to use integer indices (pointers) to access elements in arrays. Intuition: an array is a collection of elements stored sequentially. Each element has an index starting from 0. A pointer is a variable that holds an index value. By incrementing the pointer, we move to the next element. We must ensure the pointer stays within bounds (0 ≤ pointer < array length). Algorithm: initialize pointer = 0; while pointer < len(array): access array[pointer]; pointer += 1. Time: O(n) for full traversal. Space: O(1) for pointer. This feeds into L4 by providing the mechanism to track position in arrays. Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices, forgetting to increment pointer. Real code pattern: i = 0; while i < len(arr): val = arr[i]; i += 1.

examples.trace: i=0 (< len=3): access arr[0]=10; i=1. i=1 (<3): access arr[1]=20; i=2. i=2 (<3): access arr[2]=30; i=3. i=3 not <3, stop.
i=0 (< len=1): access arr[0]=5; i=1. i=1 not <1, stop.

testCases.input: array = [7, 14, 21], i=1, access then increment twice
